content:
Member-only story

Bun

JavaScript

Typescript

Software Engineering

DevOps

How Bun.$ quietly eliminated rimraf, cross-env, zx, shx, and most cross-platform shell hacks.

9 min read
1 hour ago

--

On January 20th, 2024, Bun shipped a shell nobody bothered to open. It arrived inside Bun v1.0.24, described in one changelog paragraph most people skimmed past on their way to the “faster than Node” benchmarks everyone actually cared about. Eighteen months later, a good chunk of the JavaScript build-tooling internet is still writing the same tutorial: how to make rm -rf and your environment variables behave the same way on a Windows CI runner as they do on your Mac.
Every senior engineer who has shipped a build script has hit the same wall. rm -rf works fine on macOS and throws 'rm' is not recognized as an internal or external command in PowerShell. The fix, for the better part of a decade, has been the same: install rimraf, install cross-env, maybe reach for Google's zx to get a whole cross-platform shell packed into a library. An entire sub-genre of blog posts exists to walk you through exactly that setup.
Here’s the part almost nobody has written about. If your team already runs on Bun, none of it is necessary anymore. Bun shipped its own shell, built into the runtime, and it makes the entire “install zx so your build…
